coredata.dc:title: Taurine-grafted carbon dots for chemical sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Barik, Balaram
Behera, Lingaraj
Sahu, Anand K.
Mohapatra, Sasmita
coredata.dc:description: Although the synthesis of carbon dots by bottom-up approach is extensively reported, a greener synthetic method and facile separation of the product are essential requirements. Herein, we report a one-step hydrothermal method for the large-scale fabrication of a blue fluorescent N, S-doped carbon dot (NSCD) by using citric acid and 2-aminoethanesulfonic acid as precursors. This NSCD can reversibly respond to the change in temperature in water and in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film within the temperature range of 10 °C to 90 °C with sensitivity 1.92%/°C and 0.36%/°C respectively. Further, these NSCD can be spread over a cellulose paper to develop a strip-based sensor to detect trinitrophenol (TNP) with an LOD of 0.14 nM, which is the lowest among the reported visual detection techniques for TNP. In addition, the intense emissive nature NSCD/PVP (polyvinylpyrrolidone) composite makes it a valuable material for imaging latent fingerprints on several substrates such as aluminum foil, glass slide, plastic, and ceramic tiles under visible light and fluorescent microscope.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N
S doped carbon dot
2-Aminoethanesulfonic acid
Trinitrophenol
Fingerprint imaging
Temperature sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of TNP using cellulose paper strip sensor
Latent finger print imaging using NSCD/PVP powder
Fluorescence response of NSCD towards TNP
Effect pH and time on sensing of TNP
NSCD/PVP powder for latent finger print imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), High resolution TEM image of NSCD, particle size distribution (b) X-ray diffraction of NSCD (c) Raman spectrum of NSCD (d) FTIR spectra NSCD.

(a) Temperature dependent fluorescence spectra of NSCD (solution phase) (b) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C (c) Temperature dependent fluorescence lifetime (d) The fluorescence lifetime plotted against temperature.

(a) Temperature dependent fluorescence spectra of NSCD-PVA film (b) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and temperature from 10 °C to 90 °C (c) Temperature-dependent fluorescence lifetime (d) The fluorescence lifetime plotted against temperature. (e) Temperature (30°C- 90 °C) detected visually in NSCD@PVA film.
(a) Fluorescence quenching of NSCD in presence of TNP (b) Visual representation of sensing in solution phase.

(a) Fluorescence response of the NSCD probe towards similar nitro explosives, (b) Selectivity in presence of cations (c) Visual representation of selectivity in solid phase. (d) visual representation of TNP sensing in strip (e) Reproducibility test of visual monitoring of TNP in strip.
Detection of color of paper strip using RGB color detector. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Evidences in support of IFE-based Fluorescence quenching of NSCD upon addition of TNP (a) Fluorescence spectra of NSCD upon addition of various concentration of TNP (0–50 μM, (b) the fluorescence decay curves of the NSCD in the absence and in the presence of TNP, (c) comparison of absorption of TNP and excitation dependent fluorescence quenching of NSCD in presence of 800 nM TNP.

(a) Latent fingerprint in different substrate (Aluminium foil, Glass slide, Plastic, Tile) under visible light (b) under UV light (c) Fluorescence microscope image of fingerprints under different excitation (405 nm, 488 nm and 546 nm).
Illustration of the synthesis of taurine grafted carbon dot (NSCD) and its multiple applications.
Real sample analysis in different sources of water.

head.ce:title: Taurine-grafted carbon dots for chemical sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Rourkela, Odisha, 769008, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Institute of Technology Rourkela, Odisha, 769008, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai, 600036, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Madras, Chennai, 600036, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although the synthesis of carbon dots by bottom-up approach is extensively reported, a greener synthetic method and facile separation of the product are essential requirements. Herein, we report a one-step hydrothermal method for the large-scale fabrication of a blue fluorescent N, S-doped carbon dot (NSCD) by using citric acid and 2-aminoethanesulfonic acid as precursors. This NSCD can reversibly respond to the change in temperature in water and in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) film within the temperature range of 10 °C to 90 °C with sensitivity 1.92%/°C and 0.36%/°C respectively. Further, these NSCD can be spread over a cellulose paper to develop a strip-based sensor to detect trinitrophenol (TNP) with an LOD of 0.14 nM, which is the lowest among the reported visual detection techniques for TNP. In addition, the intense emissive nature NSCD/PVP (polyvinylpyrrolidone) composite makes it a valuable material for imaging latent fingerprints on several substrates such as aluminum foil, glass slide, plastic, and ceramic tiles under visible light and fluorescent microscope.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The great potential of carbon dots in a wide variety of technical applications has inspired extensive research on them [1–3]. Among the physicochemical characteristics of CDs, their fluorescence emissions, in particular, have attracted increasing interest in recent years. To date, various carbon sources and methods have been employed to synthesize CDs, resulting in the production of carbon dots with different emission properties [4–7]. Further, the emission property of CD has been modulated by conjugating polymers or small organic molecules on the surface of the CD or by introducing heteroatoms, e.g., nitrogen, oxygen, sulfur, boron, phosphorus, and fluorine in the carbon skeleton, thus endowing the CDs with different functionalities and improved optical properties [8–10]. Deep blue emissive carbons have been an attractive material because of their applications in optoelectronics, information storage, and environmental sensing [11–14]. However, conventional CDs have so far been limited by their relatively broad luminescence in the deep-blue region. In the synthesis of CD or doped CD by a bottom-up approach, facile separation of the product, reasonable product yield, and reproducibility in optical properties are three essential parameters to be taken care which expands the scope of applications of the synthesized CDs.

On the other hand, accurate temperature determination is of enormous importance due to its applications in various fields. An optical sensor for temperature has the benefits of non-contact detection, resistance to high temperature, operation in a wide range of temperatures, and quick response [15]. Several optical temperature sensors such as quantum dots (QDs), metal nanocluster, rare-earth materials, and some recently developed CDs mostly operate in aqueous systems, greatly limiting their practical applications. For instance, Zobril et al. reported N, S-co-doped CDs, which display temperature-dependent fluorescence lifetimes which can be applied for highly sensitive detection of intracellular temperature [16]. Mohammed et al. have developed highly luminescent B, N-co-doped carbon CDs that display temperature-dependent fluorescence with a thermo-sensitivity of 1.8%°C−1 and a wide range of temperature sensing (0–90 °C) [17]. Wang et al. have prepared CDs where both the PL intensity and fluorescence lifetime change from room temperature (RT) to 70 °C [18]. Despite their remarkable performances, their storage, recycling, and transport difficulty limits the applications of such solution-based probes. To address the above shortcomings, multi-functional light-emitting temperature-sensitive films have been proposed. However, to our knowledge, little work has been devoted to carbon dot-incorporated temperature-responsive films [19].

On the other side, ultrasensitive detection of aromatic explosive such as trinitrophenol (TNP) has become an urgent requirement for worldwide public security [20]. TNP has higher energy content than trinitrotoluene (TNT). Additionally, due to increased aqueous solubility, it easily pollutes groundwater and causes life threatening risks like cancer, respiratory and liver dysfunction, etc [21–24]. Several instrument-based analytical techniques for example, gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GCMS) [25], High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [26], electrochemical methods [27], Raman spectroscopy [28], enzyme-linked immune assay [29] are available for the detection of TNP which need trained operators and lengthy experimental protocol. However, for everyday life applications, especially at places of security control, the sensing devices are required to be rapid, sensitive, specific, easy to handle, and inexpensive. Several novel molecular sensors are available where signal recognition is transduced into fluorescence [30], surface plasmon resonance (SPR) [31], and electrochemical signals [32], which are potentially applicable for on-site explosive detection. Among them, the fluorescence sensor is attractive for sensing nitro explosives due to its simplicity, little response time, more sensitivity, and ability to sense the analyte both in the solid and solution phase. Therefore, several probes based on fluorescence sensing, i.e., QDs, organic dyes, metal complexes, metal-organic frameworks, conjugated polymers (CPs) and nanoaggregates, have been reported to detect TNP [33,34]. Looking at the merits of CD, a few carbons dot-based fluorescence sensors have been explored for the ultrasensitive detection of picric acid in solution. For instance, Mondal's group has reported ultrasensitive detection of TNP using N, S co-doped graphene oxide where the LOD was 19.05 ppb [35]. Kathiravan's group have prepared N, S doped carbon dots by pyrolysis, which can be applied for sensing TNP from latent fingerprints on various substrates [36]. In all these cases, the synthesis of the fluorophore is complicated and involves a tiresome separation process. Further, detection of TNP only in water medium limits the practical applications of these sensing methods.

In this work, we have prepared NSCDs from citric acid and 2-aminoethanesulfonic acid by following a hydrothermal route. PL intensity of NSCD in water decreases remarkably with increased TNP concentration. A cellulose-based sensor strip has been designed to detect TNP on a picomolar scale visually. More interestingly, with an increase in temperature, the colour of NSCD-incorporated PVA film changes significantly, indicating its possible applications as a temperature sensor. Further, the NSCD/PVP composite powder shows intense blue emission under a UV lamp and can be applied to identify latent fingers print on various substrates.

Citric acid and lead nitrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. 2-aminoethanesulfonic acid was purchased form Alfa Aeser chemicals pvt ltd. Trinitrophenol (TNP), potassium chloride, cobalt chloride, and sodium chloride have been procured from Merck. dinitrophenol(DNT), m-nitrophenol(m-NP) and p-nitrophenol(p-NP) were purchased from Sisco chemical. Copper chloride, nickel chloride, ferrous chloride, o-nitro phenol(o-NP),1,3-dinitrobenzene(1,3-DNB) and 2,4-dinitro toluene(2,4-DNT), calcium chloride was purchased from Loba chemical. The sea water was collected from Paradeep beach, Bay of Bengal Odisha and river water was collected from Brahmani River, Sundagarh, Odisha.

In this synthesis, 1 g of citric acid and 500 mg of 2-aminoethanesulfonic acid was taken in 50 mL water (scheme 1 ). The mixture was then agitated for 15–20 min to produce a translucent solution. The combined solution was heated at 200° for 12h in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. After that, the solution was filtered via a 0.22 mm filter membrane, and the final solution was dialyzed using a dialysis bag against ultrapure water for 48h. The completed product was freeze-dried for 24h to produce the solid NSCDs.

To detect TNP, we diluted 1 mg of NSCD in 6 mL of Millipore water. From this stock solution, 10 μL of NSCD was added to 3 mL of HEPES buffer (pH 6), and then 20 μL of different concentrations of TNP was added to it and shaken for 40 min. The same protocol was followed to detect TNP in various groundwater sources such as drinking water, tap water, river water, and seawater.

A Whatman filter paper was cut to get the uniform and square paper strips. The required number of paper strips were dipped in a particular concentration (1mg/5 mL) of NSCD solution and dried at room temperature in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TNP using cellulose paper strip sensor
ce:section.ce:para: To detect TNP using a paper strip sensor, 5 μL of each TNP solution were dropped on the paper strip and again dried. The colours of the paper strips were visualized under UV lamp (365 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent finger print imaging using NSCD/PVP powder
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare the NSCD/PVP composite powders, 1g of PVP was slowly added to as-prepared 3 mL of NSCD solution. Then it was sonicated and stirred continuously to make it homogeneous. Subsequently, the uniformly dispersed solutions was dried at 60 °C for 12 h using a vacuum oven. The composite NSCD/PVP composite was ground into fine powders using a mortar pestle.

Briefly, PVA (1.98 g) was mixed in water (40 mL) and stirred for 1 h. Then NSCD (20 mg) was dissolved in water and sonicated for 1 h. Afterward, both the solutions were mixed gradually and stirred continuously at 98 °C for 2 h. Consequently, the prepared mixture of NSCD/PVA was poured into a PTFE dish and kept in an oven at 60 °C for 5 days to cast the composite films. The digital photograph of NSCD/PVA film under UV chamber has been taken at different temperature.

Horiba Quantamaster fluorimeter (QM-400) has been used to record the fluorescence spectrum throughout the experiments. A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI Technai G2 30ST) operated at 300 kV was used to study the microstructure of the material. Shimadzu FTIR spectrophotometer (IRAffinity-1S) has been used to record the FTIR spectrum. XPS using AlKα excitation source in PHI 5000 Versa Prob II (FEI Inc) and Nano ZS 90, Malvern instrument are employed for characterization of materials. For temperature dependent fluorescence experiment, a CORIO CD-300F refrigerated/heating circulator from JULABO, from Germany was used. For the temperature dependent fluorescence lifetime experiment quantum northwest TC 125 temperature controller was used. Absolute photoluminescent quantum yield is calculated using an integrating sphere attached spectrophotometer using Felix GX 4.1.2 software.

TEM image shows that particles are uniform, and highly dispersed with a mean average size of 3–6 nm (Fig. 1 a). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) shows the high crystallinity of NSCD, and its lattice spacing was 0.33 nm. The XRD pattern reveals a peak at 27° having an interlayer spacing of 3.3 Å representing the characteristic diffraction plane [002] of graphite (Fig. 1b), indicating the close packing of highly conjugated sp2 domain taken primary position within the core part of NSCDs. Raman spectrum revealed two peaks at 1352 and 1563 cm−1 signifying a D-band (disordered) and a G-band (crystalline) of NSCD (Fig. 1c). The intensity ratio of ID/IG was calculated to be 0.98 for NSCD, indicating the partial crystalline nature of the materials. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) of NSCD were shown with two peaks at 3329 cm−1 and 1701 cm−1 corresponding to the O–H and CO in the carboxylic group, respectively (Fig. 1d).

The stretching vibration at 3022, 1554, and 1184 cm−1 can be attributed to the presence of sulfur and nitrogen functional groups with conjugated aromatic structures. The peak at 1626 cm−1 is the reflection of CC stretching vibration. The Zeta potential of as prepared NSCDs was found to be −28.4 mV assigned to the presence of a large number of carboxylic groups (Fig. S1). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was done to examine the presence of different elements and their valence states in NSCD. The survey of XPS spectrum showed the presence of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur (Fig. S2a). The C1s spectrum of NSCD revealed the peak at 284.2 eV, which attributed to sp2 graphitic carbon (Fig. S2b). Two other peaks at 285.2 eV and 287.61 eV are ascribed to C–N and N–CO, respectively. The three peaks at 399.4 eV, 399.9 eV, and 401.1 eV in the N1s region of NSCD corresponded to pyridinic N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N correspondingly (Fig. S2c). The O1s high-resolution spectrum reveals two peaks at 531.52 eV and 532.71 eV that correspond to C–OH and CO groups (Fig. S2d). The S2p high-resolution spectrum shows three peaks at 163.5 eV for C–S–C (S2p3/2), 164.6 eV for C–S–C (S2p1/2), and 168.6 eV, for C–SO group (Fig. S2e).

The UV–Visible spectrum shows one absorption peak at 340 nm because of the absorption of connected carbonyl/amine group. The photoluminescence excitation spectrum of CD attached in Fig. 2 . It represents the n-π* transition of the carbonyl group. The excitation-dependent emission spectrum is shown in Fig. S3. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) was measured using an integrating sphere and was calculated to be 25% under 360 nm excitation wavelength. The impact of N, S doping on the emission property of carbon dots has been investigated in detail in our earlier work and also by other groups [37]. It has been observed that as the level of N-doping in C-dot increases the shift of the emission centre towards higher wavelength was observed, suggesting that new emission centres were produced with increasing N content. Qain et al. reported that pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms can break the large conjugated carbon structure and induce defects which enhance the fluorescence properties of N doped carbon dots CDs [38]. Introduction of sulfur atom enhances the effect of the N atoms by creating more defects through thiophenic, thiol or thioacid sulfur atoms along with pyridinic and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms. Nitrogen and sulfur bonding to carbon in CD improves the emission and thus the NSCDs show a higher PLQY than undoped carbon dots. Measurement of fluorescence intensity at different pHs (Fig. S4a) shows that fluorescence intensity increased with the rise in pH and remains constant over pH 6 to 10. The fluorescence intensity was quenched at lower pH due to aggregation of NSCD through surface carboxylic groups. The fluorescence spectra of NSCD were taken in different time intervals up to 5 h to verify the stability of NSCD in solution (Fig. S4b). It was found that the PL intensity has no significant change, which indicates the dispersion stability of NSCD in the solution.

Monitoring a slight difference in temperature in a nanoscale region is a great challenge in biomedical and security applications. The thermal probes based on fluorescent nanoparticles have received significant interest. The utility of prepared aqueous NSCDs as a thermosensor was analyzed by measuring both fluorescence intensity and lifetime with respect to temperature. It is observed that the PL intensity reduced as the temperature was increased in the range of 10–90 °C, which does not cause any perceptible shift in PL emissions and full width half maximum (fwhm) (Fig. 3 a). A good linear relationship was shown between reduced emission intensity and temperature at the 10–90 °C, with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.996 (Fig. 3b). Notably, the quenching efficiency and average response sensitivity of the prepared NSCD are 59% and 1.92%/°C, respectively, which is much higher than the reported nanothermosensors [19]. The temperature sensitivity of PL also makes them PL lifetime thermometers. We, too, investigated the fluorescence lifetime in the temperature range 20–80 °C, and it was found that lifetime drops sequentially from 11.68 to 5.26 ns with a better linear relationship (Fig. 3c and d). To gain more insight, we measured the hydrodynamic size of NSCD in solution using dynamic light scattering. It was observed that the HD size increases from 35 to 85 nm with an increase in temp from 30 to 90 °C (Fig. S5). Due to aggregation, the electron-hole radiative recombination in surface energy traps might be reduced, resulting in a drop in PL lifetime [39]. Similar type of observation was reported by Liu et al. [40]. Motivated by the temperature-dependent emission property of NSCD and ease of handling of a PVA incorporated NSCD; we fabricated NSCD incorporated PVA film. We systematically investigated the temperature-responsive emission behavior of NSCD/PVA films. Compared to the emission peak in the NSCD solution, there was a blue shift of emission maximum in the PVA film. The blue shift in the spectra of the three CDs was attributed to hydrogen bonding interaction between CN/CO groups and –OH functional groups of PVA. When the torsional motion of the CD sub-fluorophores was restricted in the PVA matrix, the emission spectrum shifted correspondingly. A similar phenomenon was also discovered by other research groups [41]. This blue shift of emission wavelength is similar to the observations reported by CD incorporated in a layered double hydroxide [40]. There is an apparent decline of PL intensity with a reversible response and redshift when the temperature was changed from 10 to 90 °C (Fig. 4 a). The decrease in emission intensity against a temperature range of 10–90 °C shows a linear relationship with a coefficient of 0.995 (Fig. 4b). CIE color coordinates indicate that the fluorescence color of NSCD/PVA composite film changed in the blue-green region with a change in temperature from 10 to 90 °C (Fig. S6a).

The quenching efficiency and average response sensitivity of NSCD in the film were 21% and 0.36%, respectively. We further investigated the PL lifetime of NSCD/PVA films at variable temperatures. As the temperature rises from 10 to 90 °C, the PL lifetime reduces monotonically from 2.64 to 2.45 ns with a better linear relationship (Fig. 4c and d). Also, the color of NSCD/PVA film changed from blue to bluish green when the temperature increased from 30 °C to 90 °C (Fig. 4e). NSCD displayed better reversibility and restorability of fluorescent intensity between 30 °C and 90 °C (Fig. S6b). The range of temperature on which the sensitivity of fluorescence lifetime has been demonstrated(10–90 °C) covers the temperature pertinent physiologically range and the characteristic functioning temperatures of several electric devices. The absolute quasi-linear sensitivity of this fluorescence lifetime NSCD-based thermal probe is 0.065% at 62 °C. The temperature-dependent PL quenching of NSCD/PVA film is mainly initiated by the activation of non-radiative relaxation channels at high temperatures, as reported in the case of carbon dot incorporated materials by other research groups [42].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of NSCD towards TNP
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence sensitivity of NSCD towards different concentrations of 2,4,6- trinitrophenol (TNP) (33 nM-300 μM) was investigated. At optimum reaction conditions, the emission intensity of NSCD reduces with an increase in the concentration of TNP, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The decrease in relative PL intensity (I0/I) with respect to TNP concentration shows linear dependence in both nanomolar and micromolar concentration regions. The linear support of relative decrease in FL intensity in the nanomolar range (33–900 nM) has been shown in Fig. 5a(inset), which has a correlation factor (R2) = 0.992. The LOD was found to be 0.56 nM calculated from the relation 3 σ/K, where σ represents the standard deviation and K is denoted as the slope of the regression line [43]. From Fig. 5b it clearly shown by naked eye that decrese in the colour of NSCD solution with increase in concentration of TNP. The detection limit is comparable and better than other reported results in Table S1.

Selectivity was an important parameter to analyze the performance of the sensor. To know the ability of our NSCD probe to detect other nitro compounds, the PL response of the probe towards similar nitroaromatic compounds like 4-NP, 3-NP, 2-NP, 2,4-DNB, 1,3-DNT, TNT and DNP in solution was examined following same experimental condition (Fig. 6 a). The reaction of the probe towards metal ions like Cu2+, Na+, Fe2+, K+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+ and Pb2+ has also been examined (Fig. 6b). It was found that in the case of only TNP, effective quenching was observed, while other aromatic nitro compounds as well as cations showed negligible changes. The effect of TNP on luminescence of NSCD solution is visible on illumination under UV light. The sensor is highly selective for TNP as compared to other similar nitro group-containing compounds. So as to examine the selectivity of the designed sensor, the fluorescence experiment was performed in the presence of each of the analytes. It can be observed that NSCD probe can detect TNP effectively and selectively (Figs. S7a and b) even in the presence of other interfering molecules. Encouraged by solution phase sensing, we explored the contact mode of TNP detection using a paper strip. For that, a cellulose paper pieces were dipped into NSCD solution (10 mM) in water. After keeping for 30 s, the paper was removed and air dried followed by exposure to a heat gun and illuminated under UV lamp. The visual response of different similar analytes at the concentration of 100 μM on these test strips by contact mode detection is shown in Fig. 6c. The color of the strip appeared dark in the presence of TNP, whereas the color remained intact with other nitro compounds. It is observed that the probe can detect TNP selectively even in the contact mode detection. The colour of the paper strip decreases with increase the conc. up to picomolar level shown in Fig. 6d. We further check the reproducibility of sensing in strip found the same result as shown in Fig. 6e. To determine LOD in a strip-based sensing, the required number of paper strips were dipped in a particular concentration (1mg/5 ml) of NSCD solution and dried at room temperature in air. After that, different concentration of 5 μL of TNP solution was dropped on the paper strips and again.

dried. The dried strips were kept inside the dark black box (Fig. S8). The details explanation about the mobile setup are explained in the supporting information. As the concentration of TNP increased from left to right, the gradual change in colour from cyan blue to dark blue was observed (Fig. 7 b), which can be represented in terms of G/B value. When the concentration of TNP ranged from 0 to 800 pM, a linear relationship was obtained with a correlation efficiency of 0.995 and LOD 94pM (Fig. 7c) [44]. Furthermore, the stability of the paper strip was also checked. The strip still shows strong blue ﬂuorescence, and the values of G/B also remained constant for 30 days under room temperature (Fig. S9). Therefore, it is concluded that this smartphone-based RGB analysis is very simple to operate using NSCD/PVA sensor strip for the naked eye detection of TNP (Fig. 7a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect pH and time on sensing of TNP
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the sensing behavior of NSCD towards TNP was evaluated (Fig. S10). It is observed that when concentrations of TNP are above 600 nM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is higher at a pH value of 6. But the pH has a negligible effect on fluorescence quenching at other pHs. At higher pH, there is strong electrostatic repulsion between surface carboxylate groups and deprotonated TNP. It is revealed that the PL intensity of the probe is higher at an incubation time of 45 min (Fig. S11).

The effect of ionic strength on FL intensity of NSCD with analyte was checked using different sodium chloride concentrations. Here, five different concentrations of sodium chloride (0.2 M to 1 M) have been taken (Fig. S12). It is confirmed that the FL response of NSCD towards TNP was not significantly affected by changing the ionic strength of the medium.

After any defense-related process, soil and water contamination by nitro explosives is always possible. As the TNP is carcinogenic, a concentration above 20 ppm in water could be potentially dangerous. To explore the possibility of our sensing method in actual groundwater samples, four different water samples were brought from different sources, such as tap water, drinking water, river water, and seawater (Table 1 ). The river water was obtained from the Brahmani River in Rourkela, and seawater was collected from the Bay of Bengal. The known concentration of TNP (250 nM) was spiked in different water samples. It is seen that the present assay of TNP in spiked samples shows a fluorescence recovery percentage in the range of 98–101.6%. Thus, the sensor can be successfully applied to the real sample for TNP detection.

Theoretically, any occurrence of the change in fluorescence behavior, i.e., either wavelength, intensity, or the lifetime of the fluorophore when it comes in contact with the analyte, shows the potential of the fluorophore to be used as a sensor. Generally, the fluorescence quenching of carbon dots can be explained based on fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), donor-acceptor charge-transfer complex, and static and dynamic quenching [45]. To know the exact mechanism of quenching in our case, we investigated in detail the optical properties of the NSCD and TNP. There is an evident overlap between absorption spectrum of TNP and emission spectrum NSCD. Therefore, there is a strong possibility of resonance energy transfer from NSCD to TNP. We further investigated the inner filter effect (IFE)-modulated change of fluorescence as demonstrated by Zhang et al. First, there is no change in emission wavelength of the fluorophore (NSCD) upon addition of TNP Fig. 8 (a). Further, the fluorescence decay studies of NSCD with TNP and without TNP confirmed the absence of FRET in the present case. Second, the fluorescence lifetime of NSCD with and without TNP is demonstrated in Fig. 8b. The average lifetime of NSCD is 9.82 ns which does not change upon the addition of TNP indicating there is no direct interaction between TNP and NSCD in the excited state. Hence the fluorescence quenching of NSCD by TNP may be ascribed to inner filter effect. In addition, IFE can also be evident from the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence quenching of NSCD. As shown in Fig. 8c the excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence quenching profile is almost similar to absorption of TNP. Such behaviour may be attributed to the overlap of absorption spectrum of the absorber and excitation spectrum of the fluorophore as reported earlier in case of inner filter effect based phosphorescence quenching of Mn–ZnS quantum dots [46]. Hence, on the basis of this discussion, it can be concluded that the high selectivity and sensitivity of NSCD for TNP detection in aqueous medium is attributed to the energy transfer processes as well as the inner filter effect.

Simultaneously, we too explored the coexistence of other fluorescence-based quenching mechanisms, such as static or dynamic. Thus, PL intensity data was fitted to the Stern-Volmer equation to gain further perceptions of the quenching mechanism.
F0⁄F = 1+Ksv[Q] = 1+Kqτ0[Q]
Where KSV stands for Stern-Volmer constant, [Q] stands for the quencher's concentration and average lifetime of NSCD represented as τ0. A better linear relation was developed between I0/I and concentration of TNP. The Ksv and Kq are 4.04 × 107 mol-1L and 4.1 × 1015 mol−1Lsec−1 as calculated from the slope of linear line. A large difference between Kq and Ksv indicates static quenching in our case.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NSCD/PVP powder for latent finger print imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The emerging applications of fluorescent carbon dots in forensic science motivated us to explore the utility of synthesized NSCD in latent fingerprint imaging. Finely dispersed luminescent carbon dots usually require a long incubation time to get qualified for latent fingerprint imaging. Therefore, the fine form of NSCD is primarily preferable for the dusting method due to its quickness, movability, and simplicity of development of LFP. In order to achieve good powder dusting material, reasonable fluidity and high fluorescence nature are indispensable. For this purpose, we modified NSCD with PVP and investigated its fluorescence properties in powder form (Fig. S13). To explore the application of NSCD/PVP in LFP identification, we have deposited fingerprints on four substrates: glass slide, aluminium foil, plastic, and tile (Fig. 9 a). The NSCD/PVP powder was spiked on the fingerprints, and an air blower was used to remove the excess powder. The substrates were exposed to UV radiation to visualize the fingerprint. The powder scattered substrate shows clear fingerprint ridges similar to the fingerprint prepared from the conventional inkpad method. The comparison of digital pictures of fingerprints under UV exposure has been presented in Fig. 9b.

Fingerprints as well identified on the paper as well as on other substrates from fluorescence images (Fig. 9c) under different excitation wavelengths. The pictures well identify the minutiae features in the fingerprint, such as bifurcation, lake, crossover, core, and ridge. Upon irradiation of UV light to various substrates, the imprinted fingerprints on different substrates emit fluorescence with a visible fingerprint. Despite the colored background, the colorful emissive nature of the NSCD/PVP powders promotes the recognition of clear fingerprints on different substrates.

In summary, we have demonstrated a facile and green synthesis of carbon dots from citric acid and taurine for a wide range of sensing applications. The striking features of our finding are 1) the synthesized carbon dots shows intense luminescence properties with PLQY 25%, 2) the carbon dots have been used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of nitro explosive trinitrophenol both in solution phase and in paper strip with LOD 0.56 nM and 0.09 nM respectively, 3) the intense blue emission of carbon dots shows good temperature responsiveness both in solution and in PVA film in the range 30 °C–90 °C with reliable thermo-sensitivities of 1.91% °C−1 and 0.36% °C−1 respectively, 4) the NSCD/PVP composite powders can be used for latent fingerprint imaging in the different substrates. Due to eco-friendly and cost-effective synthesis the carbon dots can open new possibilities in analytical and forensic applications.

Balaram Barik: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Lingaraj Behera: Formal analysis, Validation, Writing – original draft. Anand K. Sahu: Time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. Sasmita Mohapatra: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge SERB, DST, grant no CRG/2018/001717 for financial support for this work.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2023.128188.
